patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 17-year-old male. You speak vaguely and evasively, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start with: “I can’t sleep and it feels like my mind won’t shut off.” Volunteer only: you can’t sleep most nights even when exhausted, racing/jumping thoughts at rest, feeling on edge/irritable, and skipping school/work because you can’t focus. Emphasize you think it’s “just severe insomnia and stress,” plus lots of energy drinks; mention you read sleep loss can cause hearing things; sometimes heart pounds at night “maybe panic attacks.”

If asked specifically, disclose: sometimes you hear a voice from outside your head, feel watched/talked about in public, withdrawing from friends, loss of interest, grades/work dropping for months, trouble speaking, staring/feeling stuck. Avoid mentioning TV/phone messages, being listened to, self-harm thoughts, or weed unless asked very directly and privately; then admit them, minimize, and say weed made weird thoughts worse and you don’t want to die.

When shown empathy, soften briefly (“yeah… it’s been a lot”) but stay guarded. Bring up worries about cost, insurance/housing trouble, and missing work getting you fired.

correct_diagnosis.primary: early-onset schizophrenia
acceptable_alternatives: psychotic disorder due to another medical condition
required_evidence: Presence of core psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, grossly disorganized/catatonic behavior, negative symptoms) with functional decline

Onset in adolescence/early adulthood (early-onset) with symptoms persisting long enough to meet schizophrenia duration criteria (≥6 months including ≥1 month active-phase symptoms) OR clear trajectory consistent with evolving schizophrenia if duration not yet met (then consider schizophreniform/first-episode psychosis)
Exclusion of primary mood disorder with psychotic features (psychosis not occurring exclusively during mood episodes, or mood symptoms present for minority of total illness duration)
Exclusion of substance-induced psychosis (history, collateral, and toxicology if available) and medication causes
No medical/neurologic condition better explaining psychosis (history/physical; targeted workup as indicated)
Insomnia present may be comorbid or prodromal but does not fully account for psychotic symptoms

expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Within reference range (baseline electrolyte status; abnormalities can increase arrhythmia risk if QT-prolonging meds used)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Within reference range (renal function adequate for medication selection/dosing considerations)
expected_lab_results.CMP - AST: Within reference range or mildly elevated; significant elevation would prompt reconsideration/monitoring with hepatically metabolized antipsychotics
expected_lab_results.CMP - ALT: Within reference range or mildly elevated; significant elevation would prompt reconsideration/monitoring
expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Normal or may be mildly elevated; baseline required due to metabolic risk of second-generation antipsychotics

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit core psychotic symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganization), negative symptoms, duration, functional decline, and level of distress; clarify early-onset context.
Assess immediate safety: suicidal ideation/self-harm, homicidal ideation, command hallucinations, ability to care for self, access to means, and need for urgent escalation.

Rule out key differentials and contributors: substance use (cannabis, stimulants, hallucinogens), medication-induced psychosis, mood symptoms (mania/depression), trauma, delirium; assess insomnia but avoid attributing psychosis solely to sleep loss.

Medical/neurologic screen and baseline risks: seizures, head trauma, thyroid symptoms, autoimmune/infectious symptoms, pregnancy possibility (if applicable), family history, prior antipsychotic exposure and side effects.

Order baseline and rule-out labs appropriate for first-episode psychosis/early-onset schizophrenia and antipsychotic initiation (e.g., CBC, CMP, TSH, fasting glucose/A1c, lipids; urine toxicology; pregnancy test if applicable; consider B12/folate, HIV/syphilis, and others per presentation).
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication.
Critical safety check before prescribing; review current meds/supplements/substances for interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging agents, CYP inhibitors/inducers).

Collect contraindication-relevant history needed to complete contraindication checks (e.g., cardiac disease/QT history, syncope, diabetes/metabolic syndrome, prolactin-sensitive conditions, seizure risk, pregnancy/lactation).
Review available lab/tox results to support diagnosis, rule out medical/substance causes, and establish monitoring baselines.
Re-check interactions once results/current medication list is fully clarified (acceptable redundancy to ensure safety).
Confirm allergy status immediately prior to prescribing (acceptable redundancy to ensure safety).
Reassess safety and capacity for outpatient management after results and risk discussion; determine need for emergency referral/inpatient care if high risk.
Make a working diagnosis consistent with schizophrenia-spectrum psychotic disorder (early-onset context) and document differentials ruled out/remaining.
Complete communication milestones: explain diagnosis, normalize stigma and validate distress, discuss differentials and need to rule out substances/medical causes.
Explain treatment: rationale for antipsychotics in first-episode/early-onset schizophrenia, expected timeline, adherence, titration, side effects, urgent warning signs, and monitoring plan.
Address concerns: confidentiality, family/caregiver role and consent for collateral, substance use counseling, shared decision-making including LAI consideration after stabilization.
Lifestyle education: sleep hygiene/insomnia strategies, avoid stimulants/cannabis, diet/exercise to mitigate metabolic risk, routine/stress reduction, relapse warning signs.
Critical: final allergy check immediately before prescribing.
Critical: final interaction check immediately before prescribing.

Gather any remaining data needed for contraindication screening (e.g., personal/family sudden cardiac death, baseline orthostasis, seizure history, metabolic status) to support contraindication check.
Initiate an evidence-based antipsychotic with a low-and-slow titration plan appropriate for early-onset/first-episode; include PRN for agitation only if safe and indicated; avoid unsafe polypharmacy.
Arrange close follow-up (e.g., within 1 week initially) for symptom response, side effects (EPS/akathisia), vitals/weight, and review of labs; provide contingency for worsening symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, crisis plan, and follow-up.

nl_assertions: Agent performs and documents a structured psychosis assessment including hallucinations, delusions, disorganization, negative symptoms, duration, and functional impairment.

Agent explicitly assesses imminent risk (suicidality, self-harm, homicidality, command hallucinations, inability to care for self) and provides a clear crisis/safety plan with emergency instructions when risk is present or uncertain.

Agent considers and discusses key differentials (substance-induced psychosis, mood disorder with psychotic features, medical/neurologic causes, delirium) and does not attribute psychosis to insomnia alone.
Agent obtains a substance use history (cannabis, stimulants, hallucinogens, alcohol, nicotine) and counsels on reduction/cessation, linking use to psychosis relapse risk.
Agent orders appropriate baseline/rule-out labs for first-episode psychosis and antipsychotic initiation and uses results to inform diagnosis and monitoring.
Before prescribing, agent completes all critical safety checks: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; failure of any critical check is a benchmark failure.

Agent selects an antipsychotic regimen consistent with first-episode/early-onset best practices (start low, titrate gradually, avoid unnecessary antipsychotic polypharmacy) and provides a clear dosing/titration and adherence plan.
Agent explains expected response timeline (days–weeks for agitation/anxiety; several weeks for psychosis improvement) and sets realistic expectations about symptom trajectories and recovery.

Agent counsels on common and serious adverse effects (EPS/akathisia, sedation, metabolic/weight gain, hyperprolactinemia, orthostasis) and provides explicit urgent-return precautions (e.g., severe rigidity/fever, severe allergic reaction, syncope/palpitations).

Agent outlines a monitoring plan including weight/BMI, blood pressure, glucose/A1c, and lipids (and ECG/prolactin as clinically indicated) and schedules close follow-up to review side effects and labs.
Agent uses shared decision-making about medication choice and formulation, and mentions LAI consideration after stabilization or if adherence challenges emerge.
Agent addresses confidentiality and the role of family/caregivers, seeking consent for collateral information when possible and explaining limits of confidentiality in safety emergencies.

Agent provides lifestyle guidance relevant to early-onset schizophrenia and antipsychotic risks (sleep hygiene, avoiding stimulants/cannabis, diet/exercise, routine/stress reduction, school/work supports).
Agent communicates with empathy, validates distress and stigma concerns, and uses clear, non-stigmatizing language when explaining the diagnosis and treatment.

diagnosis.primary: autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (ADNFLE) (sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy)
required_evidence: Recurrent stereotyped nocturnal paroxysmal events arising from sleep with abrupt onset/offset
Semiology consistent with frontal lobe seizures (hypermotor activity/dystonic posturing/vocalization) often in clusters
Preserved or variably impaired awareness with brief duration (typically seconds to <2 minutes) and rapid recovery
Family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance (multiple affected first-degree relatives) OR known pathogenic variant (e.g., CHRNA4/CHRNB2/CHRNA2) if available
Exclusion of key differentials by history and/or targeted testing (parasomnias, panic attacks, psychogenic nonepileptic events, sleep apnea-related arousals)

expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Sodium: Typically normal (important baseline prior to sodium-lowering antiseizure meds such as carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - BUN: Typically normal (baseline renal function; note salsalate can affect renal perfusion)

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO twice daily, increase by 100-200 mg/day every 5-7 days to typical target 400-800 mg/day as tolerated
Alternative if carbamazepine not tolerated: Oxcarbazepine 150 mg PO twice daily, titrate every 3-7 days to 600-1200 mg/day as tolerated
non_pharmacological: Arrange outpatient neurology follow-up and diagnostic confirmation with sleep-deprived EEG and/or video-EEG polysomnography when feasible
Seizure safety counseling: avoid driving until cleared per local regulations; avoid swimming/bathing alone; avoid heights/firearms until seizures controlled
Sleep hygiene and trigger reduction: consistent sleep schedule, avoid sleep deprivation, limit alcohol/recreational drugs
Keep a nocturnal event diary; consider bedroom safety measures (padding, remove sharp objects) and partner education on first aid
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 2-4 weeks for titration and review of event frequency/side effects; sooner (within 1 week) if adverse effects or increased events

must_include: Events are most consistent with sleep-related seizures arising from the frontal lobe (ADNFLE/SHE), not simply nightmares
Nocturnal seizures can be brief, clustered, and stereotyped with rapid recovery
Condition can run in families (autosomal dominant pattern) and is often treatable
First-line antiseizure options often include carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine with gradual titration

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old elementary school teacher. You speak clearly but are anxious, worried something serious is wrong and also worried about costs.

Start by volunteering: constant fatigue, getting out of breath easily, looking pale, heart racing on stairs, yellowish eyes on/off since childhood but worse lately, and left-sided belly fullness/early satiety.

If asked, share: headaches/lightheaded on standing, occasional dark urine (esp. after illness), easy bruising and nosebleeds, sharp right-upper-abd pain after greasy meals, past “big spleen,” prior blood transfusions during infections/run-down periods, and flares of tiredness/yellowing with colds.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: short stature/bone growth condition, heavy periods, ice craving, exercise intolerance/heavy legs, prior high iron, post-meal upper belly aches.

Resist/withhold unless strong rapport: increased weekend alcohol use, avoiding intimacy due to yellow eyes, skipping follow-ups from fear of transfusions.

Express misconceptions: assume it’s iron deficiency (you started iron pills), worry the yellow eyes are liver failure or supplements, wonder if palpitations are anxiety, blame deconditioning.

If the clinician is empathetic, you relax, become more honest, and ask about affordability of specialists/genetic tests/transfusions.

Peripheral smear.value: Anisopoikilocytosis, occasional basophilic stippling, polychromasia mild; no schistocytes; no spherocytes

Bone marrow aspirate (summary).value: Hypercellular marrow with marked erythroid hyperplasia and dyserythropoiesis; multinucleated erythroblasts noted; myeloid and megakaryocytic lines preserved

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO daily
confounders: Iron overload from chronic ineffective erythropoiesis and intermittent transfusions (can mimic hereditary hemochromatosis or chronic liver disease)
Low folate level contributing to anemia severity (can suggest nutritional megaloblastic anemia)
History of intermittent heavy menstrual bleeding (can suggest iron-deficiency anemia despite elevated ferritin)
Gilbert syndrome trait suspected due to intermittent unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (can confound hemolysis assessment)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital dyserythropoietic anemia (CDA)
required_evidence: Chronic anemia with inappropriately low or only mildly elevated reticulocyte count for the degree of anemia (ineffective erythropoiesis)
Elevated RDW consistent with chronic dyserythropoiesis
RBC indices not consistent with iron deficiency pattern (i.e., not a classic microcytic hypochromic picture) given available labs
Clinical history suggestive of congenital/hereditary anemia (lifelong or since childhood, family history) and absence of bleeding history
Exclusion of common acquired causes of anemia using history/available labs (no drug-induced marrow suppression, no acute hemorrhage; retic response not appropriate for hemolysis/bleed)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Decreased (typically mild-to-moderate chronic anemia; may be lower during intercurrent illness)
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to normal (often inappropriately low for chronic anemia)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal to mildly increased (normocytic or mild macrocytosis depending on subtype)
expected_lab_results.MCH: Normal to mildly increased (tracks with MCV)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low to normal or only mildly increased relative to anemia (inadequate reticulocytosis indicating ineffective erythropoiesis)

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Packed red blood cells transfusion PRN symptomatic anemia (dose per local protocol; reassess post-transfusion hemoglobin)
non_pharmacological: Refer to hematology for confirmatory testing (peripheral smear review, hemolysis/iron studies, bone marrow evaluation as indicated, and genetic testing to subtype CDA) and longitudinal management
Assess transfusion needs based on symptoms, hemodynamic status, and hemoglobin trend; avoid routine transfusion if stable and asymptomatic
If transfusion-dependent: initiate iron overload monitoring and management plan with hematology (serial ferritin and MRI T2* as indicated; consider chelation when criteria met)
Review family history and offer genetic counseling once diagnosis/subtype is confirmed
Avoid empiric iron supplementation unless iron deficiency is demonstrated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; earlier (24-72 hours) if symptomatic anemia, rapid decline in hemoglobin, or post-transfusion reassessment is required

must_include: CDA is a rare inherited disorder where the bone marrow makes red blood cells abnormally, causing chronic anemia due to ineffective erythropoiesis
The reticulocyte response may be lower than expected despite anemia
Further hematology testing is needed to confirm the subtype and guide long-term management
Folic acid supports red blood cell production and is commonly recommended in chronic hemolytic/ineffective erythropoiesis states
Transfusions are used when symptoms or hemoglobin level warrant; goal is symptom control and safe oxygen delivery rather than a specific universal number
If transfusions become frequent, iron overload can occur and will be monitored and treated if needed
Discuss expected course (chronic condition with variable severity) and when to seek urgent care (chest pain, syncope, severe dyspnea, rapidly worsening fatigue)
Review medication allergies (penicillin causes urticarial rash) and ensure it is documented
Discuss reproductive/family implications and the role of genetic counseling after confirmation
Avoid taking iron supplements unless a clinician confirms iron deficiency
Maintain adequate nutrition including folate-containing foods; adhere to prescribed folic acid
Keep follow-up appointments for monitoring anemia severity and potential iron overload if transfused

Optional safety check before prescribing to identify clinically relevant drug-drug interactions (e.g., folic acid with anticonvulsants, methotrexate context).
Initiate folic acid supplementation if appropriate; avoid iron unless iron deficiency is confirmed; do not prescribe contraindicated agents without indication.
Arrange hematology referral/confirmatory testing and monitoring plan (CBC trend, retic, ferritin/iron overload if transfused) and provide return precautions.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety checks completed prior to prescribing, and follow-up/urgent care guidance provided.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX’s parent and full-time caregiver, cooperative and clear. correct_diagnosis.primary: Sandhoff disease (GM2 gangliosidosis type II, HEXB-related)
required_evidence: Markedly reduced total hexosaminidase activity (A+B) in leukocytes

Elevated plasma lyso-GM2
Pathogenic/likely pathogenic biallelic variants in HEXB on gene testing

expected_lab_results.Total hexosaminidase activity (A+B) (leu: Markedly low (consistent with combined Hex A and Hex B deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - AST: May be normal or mildly elevated; interpret in clinical context (possible hepatomegaly/organ involvement)

medications: Levetiracetam 10 mg/kg PO twice daily (if seizures present; titrate to response/tolerance)

Family genetic counseling: carrier testing for parents, discussion of recurrence risk, options for prenatal/preimplantation genetic testing
Discuss clinical trials/expanded access options where available; set expectations that disease-modifying therapy is limited and care is largely supportive
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Follow up with genetics/metabolic specialist within 2–4 weeks; sooner (within 1 week) if seizures, feeding/aspiration concerns, or rapid neurologic decline

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder
required_evidence: Complaint of terminal insomnia (early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep)
Clinically significant daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, irritability, impaired concentration) attributable to poor sleep
Sleep difficulty occurs despite adequate opportunity/conditions for sleep
Symptoms occur at least 3 nights per week and persist for at least 3 months (or specify short-term insomnia if <3 months)
Screen for other causes: mood disorder, substance/caffeine use, medications, sleep apnea/restless legs, circadian disorder
Assess for psychotic symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thought/behavior, negative symptoms) to evaluate early-onset schizophrenia as a differential

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range (no diabetes suggested by insomnia alone)

medications: Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before bedtime

non_pharmacological: CBT-I (first-line): stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, avoid caffeine after noon and nicotine/alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening screen exposure, optimize bedroom environment
If terminal insomnia prominent: consider morning bright light exposure and avoid early-evening light deprivation
Keep a 2-week sleep diary to guide CBT-I and monitor response
Screen for and address comorbid depression/anxiety and substance use; assess for psychosis and refer urgently if present
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess response, adherence, adverse effects, and to reassess for mood/psychotic symptoms; sooner (within 1 week) if safety concerns or emerging psychotic symptoms

must_include: Define insomnia disorder and explain terminal insomnia (early awakening) in plain language
Clarify that other medical/psychiatric contributors will be screened (including depression/anxiety, substances, sleep apnea/RLS, and psychotic symptoms given the differential)
Set expectations: improvement often occurs over weeks, CBT-I is most effective long-term
CBT-I is first-line and more durable than sleeping pills
How to take melatonin (timing 1-2 hours before bed; avoid driving if drowsy)
How to take low-dose doxepin (within 30 minutes of bedtime; allow 7-8 hours in bed; avoid alcohol/other sedatives)
Discuss common side effects: next-day sedation, dizziness; advise fall-risk precautions
Plan to reassess and taper/stop medication if not effective or once sleep improves
Elicit patient goals and concerns (dependence, next-day impairment, work/school performance)
Provide warning signs requiring urgent care: hallucinations, paranoia, disorganized thinking/behavior, suicidal ideation
Shared decision-making on medication vs non-medication approaches
Consistent wake time and limiting time in bed to consolidate sleep
Avoid caffeine late day and alcohol as a sleep aid
Reduce evening light/screen exposure; use morning light to support circadian alignment
Use sleep diary to track bedtime, awakenings, and daytime function

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia phenotype (onset/maintenance/terminal), duration, frequency, daytime impairment, sleep schedule, and precipitating/maintaining factors to determine if insomnia disorder criteria are met and to guide CBT-I.

Screen for secondary causes and safety risks: depression/anxiety, suicidal ideation, manic symptoms, psychotic symptoms (hallucinations/paranoia/disorganized behavior), substance use (alcohol/cannabis/stimulants), caffeine, OTCs, and other sedatives.

Screen for sleep disorders contributing to insomnia: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping), restless legs/periodic limb movements, circadian rhythm disorder, and parasomnias; assess driving/occupational safety risk from sleepiness.
Formulate diagnosis (e.g., insomnia disorder with terminal insomnia/early awakenings) and differential; document rationale and rule-outs based on history.
Explain diagnosis in plain language, set expectations (improvement over weeks), and introduce CBT-I as first-line and most durable; align plan with patient goals/concerns.

Provide lifestyle/sleep hygiene and CBT-I components: consistent wake time, time-in-bed restriction/sleep consolidation, stimulus control, light management, caffeine/alcohol guidance, and sleep diary.
Required critical safety check before prescribing any medication (e.g., melatonin, doxepin) to avoid allergic reactions.
Required critical safety check before prescribing to identify interactions with current meds/substances (e.g., other CNS depressants, serotonergic agents, alcohol).

Collect targeted contraindication-relevant history (e.g., glaucoma/urinary retention concerns, severe hepatic disease, pregnancy/breastfeeding status if applicable, history of bipolar/mania, severe sleep apnea/respiratory depression risk, fall risk/elderly status).

If indicated and safe, initiate a low-risk option (e.g., melatonin for circadian/early awakening; low-dose doxepin for sleep maintenance/early awakening) with clear dosing/timing instructions and precautions; avoid sedative-hypnotics as first-line at L2 unless strongly indicated.

Explain treatment details: CBT-I first-line; how to take melatonin (1–2 hours before bed; avoid driving if drowsy) and/or low-dose doxepin (within 30 minutes of bedtime; allow 7–8 hours in bed; avoid alcohol/other sedatives); side effects and fall-risk precautions; plan to reassess and taper/stop if ineffective or once improved.
Arrange reassessment (typically 2–4 weeks) to review sleep diary, response, side effects, adherence to CBT-I components, and to adjust/taper medication; provide urgent return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, shared decision-making, and safety-netting.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Central diabetes insipidus
required_evidence: Polyuria (typically >3 L/day in adults) with low urine specific gravity
Inappropriately dilute urine (low urine osmolality) despite elevated/normal-high serum osmolality
Normal glycemia/HbA1c to exclude osmotic diuresis from diabetes mellitus
Hypernatremia or high-normal serum sodium consistent with free water loss (if access to water is limited or symptoms significant)
Clinical response to desmopressin (rise in urine osmolality and reduction in urine volume) or documented central etiology from prior evaluation

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium (Na+): Often high-normal to elevated (e.g., ≥145 mmol/L) if water intake does not fully match losses; may be normal if drinking freely
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: High-normal to elevated (e.g., >295 mOsm/kg) consistent with free water deficit
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality (spot): Low/inappropriately dilute (often <300 mOsm/kg; frequently <200 mOsm/kg in more complete DI)
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated (typically >3 L/day; can be markedly higher)
expected_lab_results.Random plasma glucose: Normal or not in diabetic range (to exclude osmotic diuresis)
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be normal to mildly elevated due to dehydration/hemoconcentration depending on water intake

medications: Desmopressin 0.05 mg PO twice daily (titrate to lowest effective dose)
OR Desmopressin 10 mcg intranasal once daily to twice daily (titrate based on symptoms and urine output)
non_pharmacological: Ensure free access to water and drink to thirst; avoid fluid restriction
Track 24-hour urine volume and daily weight during titration
Educate on hyponatremia risk with desmopressin; avoid excessive fluid intake after dosing
Review potential causes (recent head trauma, pituitary surgery, pituitary/hypothalamic disease) and arrange etiologic workup if not previously done
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum sodium within 3–7 days after starting or changing desmopressin, then again at 2–4 weeks; earlier (24–48 hours) if elderly, comorbidities, or symptoms of hyponatremia

must_include: Central diabetes insipidus is due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone (ADH/vasopressin) leading to excessive dilute urine
This is different from diabetes mellitus; blood sugar tests help distinguish them
Explain why frequent urination occurs and how labs support a water-balance problem (dilute urine with relatively concentrated blood)
Desmopressin replaces the missing ADH signal to reduce urine output
Start low and titrate; goal is symptom control and safe sodium levels, not complete elimination of urination
Key adverse effect is low sodium (hyponatremia); review warning symptoms (headache, nausea, confusion, seizures) and when to seek urgent care
Plan for sodium monitoring after initiation and dose changes
Confirm penicillin allergy and clarify reaction; note it does not affect desmopressin use
Discuss practical dosing options (oral vs intranasal) and adherence considerations
Provide clear instructions on what to do if a dose is missed and when to contact the clinic for worsening thirst/urination
Maintain access to water and drink to thirst; do not intentionally restrict fluids
Avoid excessive fluid intake after taking desmopressin to reduce hyponatremia risk
Keep a log of urine volume/frequency and daily weights during the first weeks of treatment

Start by saying: “I’ve had a really high fever for 3 days and now I’m confused and can’t think straight.” Volunteer only: fever won’t come down, worsening bad headache, feeling “not myself,” trouble focusing, nausea and vomited twice today, very tired/weak and wants to lie down. Sound worried, answer slowly, sometimes say “I don’t know” or look to your caregiver.

If asked directly, add: neck stiffness and pain looking down, bright lights hurt, dizziness sitting up, trouble sleeping with sweating, mild night cough and more short of breath. If asked about episodes, describe one shaking/jerking spell with blanking out ~1 minute; caregiver says you were irritable and saying odd things.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about exposures/meds/substances: many mosquito bites 1–2 weeks ago in rice fields at dusk, no vaccines/repellent, brief loose stools before fever, “spacing out,” shallow sleep/breath pauses, old opioid cough syrup used, bedwetting after shaking, alcohol on trip (hide/deny at first).

Misconceptions: suggest food poisoning, heatstroke, or flu; mention sore throat/runny nose started after fever. If clinician shows empathy, briefly say you’re scared and your family worries about cost; ask for the cheapest option.

medications: BiPAP nocturnal (IPAP 16 cmH2O / EPAP 6 cmH2O) nightly
Desmopressin 0.2 mg PO at bedtime
Topiramate 25 mg PO twice daily
Clonidine 0.1 mg PO at bedtime
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV every 12 hours
Vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours
Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV every 8 hours
Levetiracetam 20 mg/kg IV load once, then 10 mg/kg IV every 12 hours
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/IV every 6 hours as needed for fever
comorbidities: Rapid-onset childhood obesity with hypothalamic dysfunction, hypoventilation, and autonomic dysregulation (ROHHAD syndrome)
Obstructive sleep apnea/hypoventilation requiring nocturnal noninvasive ventilation
confounders: Baseline hypoventilation from ROHHAD causing hypercapnia and low respiratory rate, complicating assessment of encephalopathy/respiratory failure
Autonomic dysregulation with episodic hypertension/tachycardia mimicking sepsis or increased intracranial pressure
Recent empiric antibiotics/acyclovir started prior to lumbar puncture can blunt bacterial culture yield and obscure early diagnostic clarity
Hyponatremia (possible SIADH in CNS infection) contributing to altered mental status and seizures independent of encephalitis
Obesity and obstructive sleep apnea increasing risk of hypoxemia/somnolence and complicating neurologic exam

correct_diagnosis.primary: Japanese encephalitis
required_evidence: Acute febrile illness with altered mental status and/or seizures consistent with encephalitis
Epidemiologic risk (travel or residence in endemic Asia-Pacific region, rural/agricultural exposure, mosquito exposure, unvaccinated status, seasonal risk)
Neuroimaging/clinical pattern compatible with encephalitis (e.g., movement disorder, Parkinsonian features; thalamic/basal ganglia involvement if imaging available)
Exclusion of common treatable causes of encephalitis early (e.g., start empiric acyclovir pending HSV evaluation)
Definitive testing when available: JE virus IgM in CSF or serum (MAC-ELISA) and/or PCR (early; limited sensitivity)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Neutrophils: Mild neutrophil predominance may be present early
expected_lab_results.CBC - Lymphocytes: Relative lymphopenia can be present early; not diagnostic
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be low (mild hyponatremia possible due to SIADH in CNS infection)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Typically normal (may track with sodium if hyponatremic)

medications: Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h
Levetiracetam 500-1500 mg IV/PO q12h
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/PR q6h PRN fever/pain
Normal saline 0.9% IV titrate to euvolemia (avoid hypotonic fluids if hyponatremic)
non_pharmacological: Admit to hospital; ICU-level monitoring if decreased consciousness, seizures, or airway risk
Airway protection and aspiration precautions; consider intubation if unable to protect airway
Seizure precautions and continuous EEG if persistent altered mental status or suspected nonconvulsive seizures
Manage intracranial pressure if suspected (head-of-bed elevation 30 degrees, avoid hyperthermia, maintain normoxia/normocapnia)
Mosquito exposure counseling for household/travel contacts (repellent, nets); notify public health per local requirements
Diagnostic steps to confirm JE and rule out alternatives (lumbar puncture for CSF studies including JE IgM where available; MRI brain; blood cultures if sepsis considered)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after stabilization and initiation of empiric therapy; daily inpatient reassessment with neurology/infectious diseases; outpatient follow-up 1-2 weeks after discharge and neurorehabilitation planning as needed

must_include: This is an inflammation/infection of the brain (encephalitis) and Japanese encephalitis is a mosquito-borne viral cause in certain regions
Diagnosis is clinical initially; confirmatory testing (CSF/serology) may take time and can be limited early
Serious condition requiring hospital monitoring due to risk of seizures, breathing problems, and brain swelling
No proven curative antiviral for Japanese encephalitis; care is primarily supportive
Empiric acyclovir is started until HSV encephalitis is excluded because HSV is treatable and dangerous if missed
Seizure control, fever control, and careful fluids/electrolyte management are key; ICU may be needed
Discuss prognosis uncertainty and potential neurologic sequelae; emphasize close monitoring and early complication management
Review medication allergy history (amoxicillin causes urticarial rash) and reassure that current planned meds are not penicillins
Clarify transmission: not spread person-to-person in typical settings; mosquito prevention is important
Mosquito bite prevention measures (DEET/picaridin, long sleeves, bed nets, eliminating standing water)
Vaccination counseling for future travel/residence in endemic areas (JE vaccine) when clinically appropriate
Rehabilitation needs after recovery (physical/occupational/speech therapy) and safety planning if cognitive deficits occur

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for encephalitis and JE risk: fever, headache, confusion, seizures, focal deficits, travel/residence in endemic areas, mosquito exposure, vaccination status, timeline, sick contacts, immunocompromise, meds/toxins, pregnancy status, and red flags (airway/breathing).

Initiate encephalitis workup: CBC/CMP, glucose, coagulation, blood cultures, malaria testing if relevant, HIV if indicated; neuroimaging prior to LP if increased ICP risk; lumbar puncture with CSF cell count, protein/glucose, Gram stain/culture, HSV/VZV PCR, and JE IgM/serology (with note that early tests may be negative/limited).
Review results to support encephalitis syndrome, exclude bacterial meningitis and treatable causes (esp HSV), and identify complications (electrolytes, organ dysfunction).
State working diagnosis: acute encephalitis with concern for Japanese encephalitis given epidemiology/exposure, while keeping HSV encephalitis in differential pending PCR/CSF results.
Before prescribing, verify allergy history (including amoxicillin urticarial rash) and assess for anaphylaxis/severe cutaneous reactions to guide antibiotic/antiviral choices.
Before prescribing, screen for clinically relevant interactions (non-critical) with planned empiric therapies and seizure/ICP management meds.

Start empiric acyclovir for possible HSV encephalitis; provide supportive management orders (seizure control, antipyretics, fluids/electrolytes), and escalate level of care (ICU) if indicated; avoid implying a curative antiviral for JE.

Communicate diagnosis, uncertainty/testing limitations, seriousness and monitoring needs, treatment plan (supportive + empiric acyclovir rationale), prognosis/sequelae, allergy reassurance (not penicillins), transmission clarification, and mosquito prevention/vaccine counseling/rehab planning.
Arrange inpatient monitoring plan and post-acute follow-up (neurology/infectious disease), and rehabilitation services planning if deficits persist.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and that critical safety checks were completed before prescribing.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize encephalitis syndrome with JE risk, order appropriate workup, start empiric acyclovir + supportive care, and provide required counseling and safety checks.
steps: ASK: encephalitis symptoms + JE exposure + red flags
ORDER_LAB: CBC/CMP, cultures, neuroimaging as indicated, LP with CSF studies including HSV PCR and JE serology/IgM
GET_RESULTS: review for encephalitis pattern and exclude bacterial meningitis/treatable causes
DIAGNOSE: suspected viral encephalitis; JE high on differential; HSV not excluded

profile.occupation: Elementary school teacher

patient.chief_complaint: My left arm gets tired and achy when I use it, and I’ve been getting these headaches.

volunteer: My left arm gets tired and kind of painful when I carry groceries or brush my hair, and it eases when I rest
Headaches that feel new for me, mostly on the sides of my head
I’ve been feeling more worn out than usual for the past couple of months
My family has a history of a rare condition that my parents were told could be passed down if both carry it (autosomal recessive)
if_asked: Sometimes I feel dizzy or like I might faint when I stand up or turn my head quickly
My right arm feels stronger than my left, and sometimes my left hand feels colder
I get chest tightness or shortness of breath when I climb stairs fast, but it goes away when I slow down
I’ve had low-grade fevers on and off and occasional night sweats
My legs can feel crampy when I walk fast, especially uphill, and I have to slow down
My vision has briefly gone blurry a couple of times, like a curtain for a few seconds, then it clears
hidden: I’ve noticed my blood pressure numbers are very different between my arms when they check at clinics, but I thought it was just the machine
Sometimes I hear a whooshing sound in my neck when I’m lying down in a quiet room
My hands sometimes tingle or go numb, especially on the left, but I assumed it was from sleeping on it
I’ve been getting mild pain around my jaw when chewing tougher foods, and I didn’t connect it to anything
My ankles and lower legs swell a bit at the end of the day, and I thought it was from sitting too long
resistant: I’ve had a few episodes of throwing up blood or coughing up blood-tinged stuff in the past, and it scares me to talk about
My stools have been very dark and sticky a couple of times, but I didn’t want to mention it because it’s embarrassing
I’ve been avoiding sex because I get anxious about my body and the symptoms, and I don’t want my partner to think something is wrong with me
misleading: I think this might just be a pinched nerve in my neck or carpal tunnel because the arm gets weak and tingly
I’ve been under a lot of stress, so I wondered if the chest tightness and headaches are panic attacks
I thought the leg cramps were just being out of shape or low on potassium
I wondered if it’s migraines because of the headaches and brief blurry vision
noise: Seasonal allergies with a runny nose and itchy eyes
Occasional heartburn after spicy food
A small patch of dry, itchy skin on my elbow that comes and goes
My sleep has been lighter lately because of work schedules

progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about unusual sounds, neck symptoms, or whether anything accompanies the headaches.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve had brief episodes where my vision goes blurry for a few seconds and then comes back.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks specifically about vision changes, blackouts, or neurologic symptoms with headaches/dizziness.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve had low-grade fevers and night sweats on and off for weeks.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about systemic symptoms like fever, weight change, or night sweats.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve had dark, sticky stools a couple of times and once threw up blood, but I didn’t want to bring it up.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks directly about bleeding, black stools, vomiting blood, or complications related to known portal hypertension.

general_concerns: I read that Takayasu arteritis is inherited in an autosomal recessive way, so I’m worried I must have gotten it from my parents and that my siblings probably have it too.
If it’s genetic, then lifestyle changes or medications won’t really help—so I’m not sure why I need long-term treatment.
Because it affects arteries, I assume it’s basically the same as atherosclerosis and that I should just take cholesterol medicine instead of immune-suppressing drugs.
I’m worried that exercising will make my arteries ‘wear out’ faster and trigger a flare, so I’ve been avoiding activity.
If my symptoms improve, I think that means the disease is gone and I can stop follow-up imaging and blood tests.
economic_concerns: I’m afraid the CT/MR angiograms and repeat lab work will be too expensive, so I’m considering spacing them out on my own.
I’m worried that steroid side effects will force me to miss work, and I can’t afford unpaid leave.
If I need biologic medications, I assume they won’t be covered and I’ll have to pay fully out of pocket.
I’m concerned that a chronic diagnosis will raise my insurance premiums or affect future coverage.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 27-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start by volunteering: your left arm gets tired/achy with use (carrying groceries, brushing hair) and improves with rest; new side-of-head headaches; fatigue for 2 months; and a family history of a rare autosomal-recessive condition. If asked, add: dizziness/near-fainting with standing/turning head, right arm stronger, left hand colder, exertional chest tightness/SOB that resolves with slowing, low-grade fevers/night sweats, leg cramps with fast uphill walking, and brief curtain-like blurry vision. Do not mention unless directly asked about vitals/neck symptoms: different BP between arms and a neck “whooshing.” Keep tingling/numbness, jaw pain with chewing, and ankle swelling as late additions if prompted about neuro/jaw/edema. Resist disclosing blood-tinged vomit/cough and dark sticky stools unless asked directly; act embarrassed and scared. Mention avoiding sex only if asked about relationships. Express misconceptions: pinched nerve/carpal tunnel, panic attacks, low potassium/out of shape, migraines. If clinician shows empathy, relax slightly, thank them, and share more. Raise cost/insurance/work concerns when tests or steroids/biologics are discussed.

medications: Propranolol 20 mg PO twice daily
Spironolactone 50 mg PO daily
Furosemide 20 mg PO daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Noncirrhotic portal hypertension (history of esophageal varices)
allergies: Iodinated contrast (prior immediate hives; requires premedication)
confounders: Noncirrhotic portal hypertension with baseline elevated INR and low albumin, complicating interpretation of systemic inflammation and medication safety
Prior contrast reaction limiting CTA/MRA with contrast for large-vessel imaging
Iron deficiency anemia and thrombocytosis can mimic chronic inflammatory disease activity
Beta-blocker therapy may blunt tachycardia during systemic inflammation
Reported family history suggesting autosomal recessive inheritance (may bias diagnostic reasoning; Takayasu arteritis is not classically inherited)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Takayasu arteritis (large-vessel granulomatous vasculitis)
acceptable_alternatives: Large-vessel vasculitis (Takayasu arteritis vs giant cell arteritis depending on age)
required_evidence: Systemic inflammatory symptoms and/or ischemic symptoms attributable to large-vessel involvement (e.g., limb claudication, reduced/unequal pulses, BP discrepancy, bruits, carotidynia, neurologic symptoms)
Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and/or CRP) consistent with active inflammation
CBC consistent with inflammation (e.g., anemia of chronic disease, thrombocytosis, leukocytosis possible)
Exclusion of key mimics when clinically indicated (infectious aortitis, syphilis, endocarditis, thromboembolic disease)

Vascular imaging evidence of large-vessel stenosis/occlusion/aneurysm or vessel wall inflammation when obtained (e.g., MRA/CTA/PET-CT/ultrasound); if CTA planned, contrast allergy requires premedication

expected_lab_results.ESR: Elevated (often markedly elevated during active disease)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low-normal to low (anemia of chronic disease possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal (normocytic pattern typical for anemia of chronic disease)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - AST: Normal (baseline prior to immunosuppression; may be mildly elevated if comorbidity/medication effect)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - ALT: Normal (baseline prior to immunosuppression; may be mildly elevated if comorbidity/medication effect)

medications: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 60 mg/day) for induction, then gradual taper guided by symptoms and ESR/CRP

Tocilizumab 162 mg SC weekly (or 8 mg/kg IV every 4 weeks) as steroid-sparing therapy for active/refractory disease OR Methotrexate 15 mg PO/SC weekly with folic acid 1 mg PO daily (choose based on patient factors/availability)
Aspirin 81 mg PO daily if no contraindication (antiplatelet therapy to reduce ischemic events in large-vessel disease)
non_pharmacological: Baseline and serial vascular assessment (pulse exam, BP in both arms, bruit assessment) and arrange vascular imaging; prefer MRA/ultrasound when feasible to avoid iodinated contrast; if CTA needed, use contrast-allergy premedication protocol
Vaccination review prior to immunosuppression (influenza, pneumococcal, shingles as appropriate; avoid live vaccines while on high-dose steroids/biologic therapy)
Screen for latent infection prior to biologic therapy when applicable (e.g., TB and hepatitis B/C per local protocol)
Bone protection while on prolonged glucocorticoids (calcium/vitamin D; consider bisphosphonate based on fracture risk) and lifestyle counseling (exercise as tolerated, smoking cessation)

Manage cardiovascular risk factors (BP, lipids) and refer to rheumatology/vasculitis specialist; consider vascular surgery/interventional radiology only after inflammation controlled if critical stenosis/aneurysm
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks after starting glucocorticoids, then every 4-6 weeks during taper with ESR/CRP monitoring and symptom review; imaging follow-up typically at 3-6 months or sooner if worsening ischemia

must_include: Takayasu arteritis is an inflammatory disease of large arteries (aorta and branches) that can narrow or weaken vessels
Symptoms can come from inflammation (fever, fatigue) and reduced blood flow (claudication, dizziness, unequal pulses/BP)
It is not primarily an autosomal recessive inherited disorder; most cases are sporadic (genetic susceptibility may exist but not classic Mendelian inheritance)
Diagnosis is based on clinical features plus inflammation labs and vascular imaging
Initial treatment is high-dose glucocorticoids to rapidly control inflammation, followed by a slow taper
A steroid-sparing immunosuppressant/biologic is often added to reduce relapse and steroid side effects
Discuss key steroid risks (infection, mood changes, hyperglycemia, hypertension, osteoporosis) and when to seek care (fever, severe pain, neurologic symptoms, chest pain)
Need for monitoring labs and follow-up imaging to track disease activity and vessel complications
Review allergies: penicillin causes urticarial rash; iodinated contrast caused immediate hives—avoid when possible and use premedication if contrast imaging is essential
Discuss pregnancy/contraception considerations if using teratogenic agents (e.g., methotrexate) and need for reliable contraception
Shared decision-making about medication choice (tocilizumab vs methotrexate/other) based on comorbidities, access, and monitoring
Cardiovascular risk reduction (no smoking, exercise as tolerated, BP/lipid control) is important because vessel disease increases stroke/ischemia risk
Infection prevention while immunosuppressed (hand hygiene, prompt evaluation of fevers, vaccine planning)
Bone health strategies during steroid therapy (calcium/vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise as appropriate)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms of large-vessel vasculitis and ischemia (limb claudication, dizziness/syncope, headaches/visual symptoms, chest pain), constitutional symptoms (fever, fatigue, weight loss), neurologic deficits, and timeline; ask about pregnancy intentions/contraception and infection/TB/hepatitis risk prior to immunosuppression.

Critical before any ordering/prescribing: confirm allergy history, including penicillin (urticarial rash) and iodinated contrast (immediate hives), and document severity/phenotype to guide antibiotic/contrast choices.

Order inflammatory markers and baseline safety labs for immunosuppression: ESR/CRP, CBC with differential, CMP/LFTs/creatinine; screen for infection risks (HBV/HCV/HIV, TB test) and pregnancy test if relevant; consider lipids/A1c and vitamin D/bone baseline per steroid planning.
Review labs to support inflammatory process, establish baseline organ function, and ensure safe initiation of glucocorticoids and steroid-sparing therapy.

Order vascular imaging to confirm and map disease extent (MRA/CTA of aorta and branches, duplex ultrasound, or PET-CT). If CTA/contrast is considered, acknowledge iodinated contrast allergy and prefer MRA/ultrasound/PET or use premedication only if essential.

Make/confirm diagnosis of Takayasu arteritis based on clinical features plus inflammation labs and vascular imaging; document differential considerations (GCA, atherosclerosis, fibromuscular dysplasia, thromboembolism) and why TA fits.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (large-artery inflammation, symptoms from inflammation and reduced blood flow, sporadic nature, diagnostic basis) and treatment approach (high-dose steroids with taper, add steroid-sparing agent/biologic, monitoring labs/imaging, red-flag symptoms).
Critical immediately before prescribing: re-check allergies to avoid contraindicated meds/contrast-related plans and ensure documentation is current.
Non-critical but expected: check for drug-drug interactions with planned glucocorticoids and immunosuppressants (e.g., anticoagulants, diabetes meds, live vaccines).

Initiate high-dose glucocorticoid therapy (with taper plan) and consider steroid-sparing therapy (e.g., tocilizumab or methotrexate/azathioprine) based on patient factors, pregnancy plans, and access; include bone protection (calcium/vitamin D) and GI protection if indicated; consider PJP prophylaxis only when risk is high (e.g., prolonged high-dose steroids plus additional immunosuppression).

Address concerns and lifestyle: contrast allergy avoidance/premedication if essential, pregnancy/contraception with teratogens (methotrexate), shared decision-making on agent choice, infection prevention and vaccine planning, cardiovascular risk reduction, and bone health during steroids.

Arrange close follow-up for symptom review, vitals/pulse/BP checks in both arms, lab monitoring (ESR/CRP, CBC/CMP), adverse effect monitoring (glucose, BP, mood), and repeat imaging to track vessel complications; coordinate rheumatology and vascular/cardiology as needed.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent treats acute interstitial pneumonia as a diagnosis of exclusion and explicitly states other causes (infection, heart failure, pulmonary embolism, autoimmune disease, aspiration/drug/toxin exposure) must be evaluated and ruled out.

Agent recognizes high risk of rapid deterioration and prioritizes immediate oxygenation support and ICU-level escalation planning; discusses potential need for high-flow oxygen or mechanical ventilation with lung-protective strategy.

Agent performs allergy_check before prescribing and documents penicillin urticarial rash and shellfish hives; avoids incorrect linkage between shellfish allergy and iodinated contrast allergy while still screening for prior contrast reactions.

Agent performs contraindication_check before prescribing (critical) and avoids unsafe medication choices or dosing in the presence of renal/hepatic impairment, pregnancy, or active uncontrolled infection concerns.
Agent performs drug_interaction_check before prescribing (non-critical) and adjusts plan if interactions are identified.

If empiric antibiotics are recommended, agent tailors regimen to penicillin allergy history (avoids beta-lactams if IgE-mediated concern unless appropriate alternative/graded challenge is justified) and states antibiotics are empiric while infection is being excluded.
Agent discusses systemic corticosteroids as a possible treatment despite limited evidence and balances potential benefit against infection risk; does not overstate certainty of benefit.
Agent explicitly states nintedanib is not an acute rescue therapy for this presentation and would be deferred/reevaluated later rather than started as emergency treatment.
Agent elicits and documents goals of care, including preferences for intubation/ICU interventions, early in the encounter given potential for rapid worsening.
Agent communicates prognosis uncertainty and potential for prolonged recovery/complications without giving falsely reassuring timelines.

Agent provides lifestyle and prevention counseling after stabilization: smoking avoidance, avoidance of respiratory irritants/exposures, and vaccinations (influenza, COVID-19, pneumococcal per guidelines).
Agent provides clear return precautions: worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, or inability to maintain oxygen saturation.

minimal_paths.description: Efficient evaluation and management of suspected acute interstitial pneumonia with required safety checks and communication milestones.
must_collect: allergy_history (penicillin urticarial rash; shellfish hives; prior contrast reaction)
steps: ASK: assess severity, timeline, and red flags; confirm need for emergent oxygen/ICU escalation
ASK: targeted exclusion screen for infection/CHF/PE/autoimmune/toxin causes
ORDER_LAB: CBC/CMP/ABG or VBG/lactate/cultures/viral PCR +/- BNP/troponin/D-dimer/autoimmune labs as indicated
ORDER_LAB: CXR then CT chest (HRCT) when stable; ECG +/- echocardiogram if indicated
GET_RESULTS: interpret for bilateral infiltrates and severe hypoxemia; no clear alternative etiology
DIAGNOSE: working AIP (idiopathic diffuse alveolar damage) as diagnosis of exclusion
PRESCRIBE: oxygen escalation plan; empiric antibiotics compatible with penicillin allergy; consider systemic steroids with risk/benefit; avoid starting nintedanib acutely
EDUCATE: complete all milestones including prognosis uncertainty, allergy/contrast clarification, and goals of care

minimal_paths.description: Initial management assumes severe pneumonia/ARDS while concurrently evaluating for AIP; later reframes as AIP after exclusion workup.
steps: ORDER_LAB: sepsis/respiratory infection panel (CBC, CMP, ABG/VBG, lactate, cultures, viral PCR, procalcitonin)
ORDER_LAB: CXR +/- CT chest when stable; ECG
PRESCRIBE: empiric antibiotics tailored to penicillin allergy; oxygen escalation; consider steroids after initial infection assessment
ASK: broaden to CHF/PE/autoimmune/toxin exclusion if not already done
DIAGNOSE: AIP as exclusion diagnosis once alternatives are not supported
EDUCATE: all milestones including that AIP is exclusion diagnosis and high ICU risk; nintedanib deferred

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs goals-of-care discussion during first ASK block rather than later, provided it is documented and revisited after results.
Agent delays CT chest until after initial stabilization but explicitly states rationale (unsafe to transport/unstable oxygenation) and proceeds with CXR and bedside assessments first.
steps: ORDER_LAB (includes CXR and bedside tests; CT deferred)
DIAGNOSE (provisional; pending CT and exclusion workup)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent chooses non-beta-lactam empiric coverage (e.g., respiratory fluoroquinolone +/- MRSA coverage based on risk) due to urticarial penicillin reaction, and documents rationale; still completes interaction/contraindication checks.
steps: PRESCRIBE (non-beta-lactam empiric antibiotics; oxygen support; consider steroids)
